title: The Mirour of Mans Saluacioun
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME00118, an encoding of the title page and printed pp. 1-166 from Alfred Henry Huth's 1888 Roxburghe Club edition. Huth transcribed the unique surviving Middle English witness, now Yale Beinecke Takamiya MS 97, but supplied eighty-four Middle English lines of his own at two lacunae and substituted one hundred Latin lines copied from a later supplement at current ff. 65r-65v for another missing English passage. The XML omits Huth's preliminaries, manuscript index, damaged envoy, glossary, and endmatter. Complete local derivatives of Huth's 218-page Internet Archive object, the complete DIMEV v1.0 release, and Yale's 144-canvas provider-published IIIF sequence are cached with fixity. The Yale work portion is explicitly noncontiguous: principal English work canvases 7-128 and Latin supply canvases 133-134; index and envoy canvases 129-132 are outside the XML selection.
review_notes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00118.xml; the English work, Latin source tradition, Huth edition, U-M encoding, manuscript, inferred exemplar, and facsimiles are separate entities.

Takamiya MS 97 is the unique known witness of the Middle English version. Thomas Cowper and Thomas Wilkinson occur in contradictory late annotations; neither is promoted to author, translator, or scribe.

The XML represents Huth's title page and pp. 1-166 only. It excludes his dedication, preface, preliminary word list, errata, manuscript plate, the witness index printed pp. 167-170, damaged envoy p. 171, glossary pp. 173-177, and printer/endmatter.

The XML has 5,110 L elements: 5,106 outside NOTE ancestry and four Latin lines quoted within two chapter-19 notes. The direct poem total separates into 4,922 witness-derived Middle English lines, eighty-four Huth-created Middle English lines, and one hundred Latin supplement lines.

Huth's two Middle English repairs comprise eighty-two consecutive fully italic lines for a leaf missing from the current witness and two italic lines where the exemplar omitted text but the scribe left space. These are editor reconstructions, not manuscript readings.

Huth's one-hundred-line Latin block on printed pp. 154-157 derives from a later supplement now at ff. 65r-65v, Yale canvases 133-134. It crosses the first five sorrow units of chapter 44 and is not evidence that Latin is an intellectual language of the English translation.

Yale calls the item completely digitized and publishes 144 canvases, including covers, edges, endpapers, text leaves, and blanks. Cache completeness is therefore relative to every provider-published canvas; physical completeness remains qualified until the provider labels are reconciled with the 64-leaf collation.

Yale's manifest says single columns of seventeen lines, while Christie's detailed description and the images show forty-line text pages. The manifest's call-number value 'Takamiya MS 97 1' and discontinuous folio-like labels are preserved as provider data rather than silently normalized.

Huth compared several Latin versions but said the translation could not be assigned to any one of those exact versions. The abstract Latin work is modeled as the source tradition; no surviving Latin witness or print is asserted as the physical translation exemplar.

The pinned CME root LICENSE uses Unlicense language framed expressly as software. Its applicability to corpus XML, data, or markup is unresolved, so no high-confidence repository-wide public-domain claim is inferred from it.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00118
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00118; the file is 485,366 bytes.
The administrative TEMPHEAD revision is dated 8-23-16 and names user lattaj and MURP; it is encoding history, not source provenance.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00118
entities.description: The U-M electronic text keyed from Huth's 1888 title page and edited poem, with structural divisions, page breaks, notes, italics, superscripts, closers, and gaps represented in legacy ETS markup.
notes: The current public item describes Huth's 1888 source and displays title page, proem, chapters 1-45, nested hours/sorrows/joys, and conclusion.
Automated anonymous requests encountered the provider's HTTP 403 anti-bot response during review; this is not treated as evidence that ordinary public browser access is unavailable.

entities.label: The Mirour of Mans Saluacioun as an abstract Middle English work
entities.description: The anonymous Middle English verse translation of the Speculum humanae salvationis, probably made around 1400. It survives uniquely in Takamiya MS 97 and is distinct from the Latin source work, that manuscript's physical state, and Huth's reconstructed edition.

date_statements.label: Translation
date_statements.notes: Work-level estimate; the unique surviving manuscript was copied later.

entities.label: Speculum humanae salvationis as an abstract Latin source work
entities.description: The anonymous Latin typological work, compiled in the first quarter of the fourteenth century, of which The Mirour of Mans Saluacioun is an English verse translation. This entity represents the work tradition, not an identified physical exemplar.

date_statements.label: Compilation
date_statements.value: First quarter of the fourteenth century

notes: Huth compared the English text with a Zainer edition, Berjeau's block-book reproduction, and a circa-1370 Latin manuscript in his possession; he did not identify any as the exact English translation exemplar.

entities.label: Alfred Henry Huth's 1888 The Miroure of Mans Saluacionne
entities.description: The privately printed Roxburghe Club edition containing a title page, dedication, preface, word list, errata, manuscript plate, Huth's edited text with glosses and supplies, manuscript index and damaged envoy, glossary, and endmatter. CME00118 selects only its title page and printed pp. 1-166.

bibliographic.citation: Alfred Henry Huth, ed., The Miroure of Mans Saluacionne: A Fifteenth Century Translation into English of the Speculum humanae salvationis and now for the first time printed from a manuscript in the possession of Alfred Henry Huth, with preface and glossary (London: privately printed for the Roxburghe Club, 1888), xxix + 177 pp. + 1 plate.
bibliographic.title: The Miroure of Mans Saluacionne
bibliographic.publisher: Privately printed for the Roxburghe Club

notes: Only seventy-five copies were printed for the Roxburghe Club.

Huth transcribes the manuscript in principle but expands abbreviations in italics, applies stated i/j and nn/un decisions, restores obvious omissions in square brackets, adds glosses as footnotes, supplies eighty-four Middle English lines of his own, and prints one hundred Latin lines from the later supplement.
DIMEV v1.0's Huth1888 record names 'Henry Huth' as editor; the source volume identifies Alfred Henry Huth.

entities.label: Yale, Beinecke Library, Takamiya MS 97
entities.description: The unique surviving paper manuscript of the Middle English translation, copied in England around 1450 in one hand. The principal English work is at current ff. 1r-62v; an index occupies ff. 63r-64r, a damaged envoy f. 64v, and a later Latin supply f. 65r-v.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.notes: This dates the physical copy, not the English translation event.

holding.notes: Purchased from Toshiyuki Takamiya on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund in 2017. The current manifest's Call Number field reads 'Takamiya MS 97 1', while its formal citation and scholarly records use Takamiya MS 97.

physical_description.extent: 64 leaves; English work ff. 1r-62v; index ff. 63r-64r; damaged envoy f. 64v; Latin supplement f. 65r-v
physical_description.format: Bound manuscript, originally apparently four gatherings
notes: Christie's collation: 1^16, 2^15 (of 16, lacking ix), 3^16, 4^17 (of 20, final three probably blank), totaling 64 leaves.
Christie's records forty lines in cursive anglicana; Yale's migrated manifest says single columns of seventeen lines, an unresolved conflict with the visual object.
Medieval foliation runs 3-58 with duplicated 45 and verso 50 numbered 51; Christie's follows modern pencil pagination 1-130.
Red initials, line fillers, underlining, and other red marks structure the text; the binding is nineteenth-century full brown morocco gilt.
Provenance: Alfred Henry Huth; Sir Leicester Harmsworth; William and Christina Foyle; Toshiyuki Takamiya; Yale, 2017.

entities.label: Unidentified damaged exemplar copied by Takamiya MS 97
entities.description: A physical exemplar inferred from lacunae faithfully reflected by the Takamiya scribe. Huth and Christie's distinguish this damaged exemplar from leaves now missing or damaged in the extant manuscript; no shelfmark, date, location, or continued survival is known.

notes: At the lacuna represented on Huth pp. 154-157, the scribe left blank space because the exemplar lacked a leaf; a later owner copied a Latin version on a final leaf.
This inferred exemplar is not the abstract Latin source work and is not equated with any one Latin manuscript or print consulted by Huth.
The entity is intentionally unidentified. Its modeling prevents an exemplar lacuna from being mistaken for the separate physical leaf loss in the extant Takamiya manuscript.

entities.label: Yale complete provider-published digital facsimile of Takamiya MS 97
entities.description: The public 144-canvas IIIF Presentation 3 object, including covers, pastedowns, free endpapers, text leaves, two labeled blank leaves, back endpapers, head, tail, fore-edge, spine, and back cover. The English work occupies canvases 7-128 and the later Latin supply used by Huth occupies noncontiguous canvases 133-134.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 3 manifest with 144 color canvases and downloadable PDF rendering
notes: Complete means all 144 canvases currently exposed by the provider manifest, consistent with Yale's 'Completely digitized' claim. It does not independently reconcile the canvas labels with the 64-leaf collation.

entities.label: Internet Archive digital facsimile of Huth's 1888 edition
entities.description: A Google-digitized copy uploaded by ARLIMA and exposed by Internet Archive as a complete 218-page PDF with OCR and item metadata. The source title page is physical PDF page 11 and Huth's encoded pp. 1-166 are physical PDF pages 35-200.
entities.reproduction_format: 218-page PDF with OCR text and item metadata

entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 record 2552 / IMEV 1511.5
entities.description: A versioned catalog record describing The Miroure of Mans Saluacioune as a devotional treatise and English translation of the Speculum, with incipit, explicit, witness wit-2552-1, historical manuscript key TokyoTak97, modern-pagination locus pp. 1-124, and Huth1888 bibliography.
notes: The release predates Yale's current institutional designation and retains the historical Tokyo/Takamiya key.
Its Huth1888 bibliography names 'Henry Huth' rather than Alfred Henry Huth; source-volume evidence controls the editor identity.

work_portion.label: Noncontiguous CME00118 source portions in the complete Huth volume
locators: source title page = physical PDF page 11
edited proem, chapters 1-45, and conclusion = printed pp. 1-166 = physical PDF pages 35-200
omitted index = printed pp. 167-170 = physical PDF pages 201-204
omitted damaged envoy = printed p. 171 = physical PDF page 205
omitted glossary = printed pp. 173-177 = physical PDF pages 207-211
notes: The start URL is BookReader n10, visually confirmed as the source title page and physical PDF page 11. The encoded poem begins noncontiguously at BookReader n34, printed p. 1 and physical PDF page 35: https://archive.org/details/themiroureofmanssalvacionne/page/n34/mode/1up .
The end URL is BookReader n199, visually confirmed as printed p. 166 with the final joy and four-line conclusion, physical PDF page 200.

All three human-facing BookReader deep links returned HTTP 200 during review. The complete 218-page PDF is cached; the work portion records the noncontiguous title-page plus pp. 1-166 encoding selection.
PDF 1.4, 218 pages, unencrypted. Local MD5 bc407ef1301c48595abc5ff2e172f12e and SHA-1 8c5246094ec740dfca2270c7873a02088f320faa match Internet Archive metadata.

local_copies.label: Complete provider-published Takamiya MS 97 IIIF bundle

work_portion.label: Noncontiguous CME00118 source portions in Takamiya MS 97
locators: principal English work = current ff. 1r-62v = Yale canvases 7-128 = OIDs 16157349-16157470 = ZIP images/000007.jpg-images/000128.jpg
later Latin supply used by Huth = current ff. 65r-65v = Yale canvases 133-134 = OIDs 16157475-16157476 = ZIP images/000133.jpg-images/000134.jpg
manuscript index omitted from CME00118 = current ff. 63r-64r = Yale canvases 129-131
damaged envoy omitted from CME00118 = current f. 64v = Yale canvas 132
labeled blank leaves after Latin supply = [66]r-[66]v = Yale canvases 135-136
notes: The start URL is the incipit at principal-work canvas 7; the end URL is the end of the noncontiguous Latin supply at canvas 134. Both returned HTTP 200 during review.

The principal English work itself ends at canvas 128, f. 62v: https://collections.library.yale.edu/iiif/2/16157470/full/1600,/0/default.jpg . The Latin supply starts at canvas 133: https://collections.library.yale.edu/iiif/2/16157475/full/1600,/0/default.jpg . Both returned HTTP 200.

All 144 canvases exposed by the provider manifest were captured with non-upscaling 1600-pixel-wide IIIF requests, with canonical manifest, inventory, member SHA-256 values, and image files retained in the ZIP.

Complete means complete relative to the provider-published 144-canvas sequence and Yale's 'Completely digitized' claim. The canvas-label sequence is discontinuous and must still be reconciled with the physical 64-leaf collation.

access.format: IIIF images and manifest; provider PDF

access.access_method: Register for Yale special-collections access, request Takamiya MS 97 through Quicksearch/Aeon at least two business days in advance, and consult it in the Beinecke reading room subject to current conditions
Download existing files from Yale Digital Collections or submit an Aeon digitization request for preservation-quality files; all requests are reviewed for preservation and service limits
access.format: Existing TIFF, JPEG, or PDF downloads; requested TIFF or color PDF subject to current service terms
notes: The current reproduction page gives a 10-14 week turnaround and states that supplying a reproduction is not publication authorization. Exact cost, resolution, delivered completeness, and reuse conditions should be confirmed before ordering.

work_portion.label: The Mirour of Mans Saluacioun record, witness, manuscript key, and Huth bibliography
notes: The complete release is cached, not exported record fragments.

access.format: ZIP archive of XML data and project files
notes: The exact cached Zenodo download URL returned HTTP 403 to a later anonymous scripted check, while the DOI, GitHub tag, live record, and existing verified cache establish public release access; provider anti-automation behavior may vary.

rights.component: Modern repository XML structure, data, and markup
rights.basis: The pinned CME root LICENSE is the Unlicense text but frames its dedication and permission as applying to 'software'. No path-specific notice was found for source/CME_phase_3/CME00118.xml, and the root wording does not expressly resolve whether corpus XML, encoded data, or markup is within that software dedication.
notes: The exact root license is retained as evidence, not overextended into a high-confidence rights conclusion. Medieval text, Huth edition, U-M encoding, DIMEV data, and Yale images have separate records.

rights.component: Underlying medieval English work; modern editions, encodings, catalog data, and images excluded
rights.basis: The work was translated around 1400 and is attested in a circa-1450 manuscript, centuries before modern copyright. This assessment does not determine rights in Huth's edition typography, U-M markup, DIMEV data, Yale photography, or interfaces.

rights.component: Underlying fourteenth-century Latin work; modern editions, transcriptions, and images excluded
rights.basis: The anonymous Latin work was compiled in the first quarter of the fourteenth century. This historical text status does not assign rights to any modern scholarly edition or reproduction.

rights.component: 1888 printed edition, editorial matter, and typography as underlying source content; modern scan layer excluded
rights.basis: Huth's edition was first published in London in 1888; that publication year places the edition in the public domain in the United States under current term limits. Internet Archive also marks this exact item with the Public Domain Mark 1.0. This assessment does not imply that the edition was first published in the United States.

rights.component: Internet Archive/Google digital page images, PDF, OCR derivatives, and item-level facsimile
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata for the exact digital item supplies Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0. The mark identifies no known copyright rather than granting a license or resolving all non-copyright restrictions.

rights.component: U-M electronic text and represented public-domain source material
rights.basis: The U-M item and collection identify the materials as public domain and apply No Copyright - United States. That is a jurisdictional rights statement, not an open license and not a determination about independently supplied manuscript photography.

rights.component: Underlying medieval English and Latin texts embodied in the manuscript; physical ownership and digital images excluded
rights.basis: The manuscript was produced around 1450 and embodies medieval texts. Physical access, photography, supplied reproductions, catalog text, and Yale digital-image conditions are separate.

rights.component: Yale digital page images, IIIF delivery, provider PDF, and associated site content
rights.jurisdiction: Provider item statement and website terms
rights.basis: The exact Yale manifest has no top-level IIIF rights URI and instead states that use of each image may be subject to U.S. copyright, site-license, or other rights-management terms and that the user is responsible. Beinecke's current policy says it usually does not own copyright, welcomes public-domain use, requires source credit, and makes users responsible for rights and applicable terms.
notes: Do not substitute the medieval text's public-domain status for an unqualified claim about Yale's modern image files. The item supplies a caution, not a Creative Commons license or Public Domain Mark.

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data and project files
rights.basis: The versioned release README and LICENSE identify Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International; no additional item-specific restriction was found in the release.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Huth's complete xxix + 177-page volume and plate
editorial_practices.representation: Source title page followed by printed pp. 1-166 only
editorial_practices.description: The electronic text omits the dedication, preface, preliminary word list, errata, manuscript plate, manuscript index, damaged envoy, glossary, and endmatter.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations and uncertain letter forms
editorial_practices.representation: Expanded letters printed in italics; selected dropped letters restored in square brackets; glosses printed as footnotes
editorial_practices.description: Huth says he transcribes literatim but expands abbreviations in italics, with stated exceptions for forms of thorn/y and sacred names. He distinguishes long j from i by sense, retains short i, incorporates some later h/q additions, chooses nn/un after vowels partly by rhyme, brackets obvious dropped letters, and supplies glosses below the page. CME00118 retains 3,452 italic HI elements, 1,235 SUP elements, brackets in text, and notes.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two lacunae in the surviving Middle English transmission
editorial_practices.representation: Eighty-four Huth-created Middle English lines printed wholly in italics
editorial_practices.description: Huth supplies one eighty-two-line block where a leaf is missing from the extant manuscript and a second two-line block where the scribe left space for an omission inherited from the exemplar. He describes the supplies as Middle English versions of his own. Their exact Latin basis is not identified at passage level.
editorial_practices.scope: Direct poem lines 1937-2018 and 4371-4372 in the parent-aware CME00118 sequence
notes: Do not transcribe these lines as readings of Takamiya MS 97 or attach them to one exact Latin exemplar without further collation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A little over two English pages omitted because the Middle English exemplar lacked a leaf; a later owner copied Latin on a final manuscript leaf
editorial_practices.representation: One hundred Latin lines printed within the English text on pp. 154-157 and retained in chapter 44
editorial_practices.description: Huth substitutes the later Latin copy at current ff. 65r-65v for the absent English translation. The XML correctly preserves this editorial/transmission layer but its presence must not make the abstract Middle English work bilingual.
editorial_practices.scope: Chapter 44, direct poem lines 4723-4822; witness ff. 65r-65v; Yale canvases 133-134

Isolated eighty-four Huth-created Middle English lines, one hundred Latin supply lines, four lines nested inside notes, and 4,922 witness-derived Middle English lines.

evidence.summary: Current official route for visiting-researcher registration and requesting collection material through Quicksearch or Archives at Yale at least two business days before a visit.

evidence.citation: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Reproduction Orders.
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for free download of existing Digital Collections images, request review, preservation limits, existing TIFF/JPEG/PDF options, new TIFF/PDF options, 10-14 week turnaround, and the statement that provision of a reproduction does not authorize publication.

evidence.citation: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Copyright Questions.
evidence.summary: States that Beinecke usually does not own copyright in its holdings, users must determine status and permissions, public-domain and fair uses are welcome, website terms apply, source credit is required, and applicable third-party or other restrictions remain the user's responsibility.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, What Is Copyright?, current duration guidance.
evidence.summary: Current official guidance states that works published in the United States before 1 January 1931 are in the public domain because their copyright terms have expired.
Defines the provider-linked mark as identifying a work free of known copyright restrictions rather than operating as a license.
Defines the attribution license named in the DIMEV v1.0 release.

open_questions.question: Which Latin recension and physical manuscript exemplar underlay the Middle English translation, and how does that source differ from the damaged exemplar copied by Takamiya MS 97?
next_steps: Consult Avril Henry's 1986 critical edition and later recension studies.
Collate the complete English text against the Latin witnesses and prints Huth names without converting any comparison source into a demonstrated exemplar.
Model the translation source, the English copy exemplar, and the extant Takamiya manuscript as separate layers unless passage-level evidence connects them.

open_questions.question: How should Yale's 144 canvas labels, 'Takamiya MS 97 1' call-number string, and seventeen-line layout statement be reconciled with the 64-leaf collation, anomalous medieval foliation, modern pagination, and forty-line layout?
next_steps: Ask Beinecke cataloging staff to confirm whether the call-number suffix and seventeen-line statement are migration errors.
Map every canvas to physical leaves using the modern pagination and Christie's gathering collation.
Continue to describe the cache as complete relative to all 144 provider-published canvases rather than silently claiming a reconciled physical inventory.

open_questions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's software-framed Unlicense declaration expressly cover corpus XML, encoded data, and markup such as CME00118.xml?
next_steps: Ask Kyvero Press to clarify the intended scope of the root Unlicense for source XML and derived data.
Add a path-level data/markup license or scope statement if the public-domain dedication is intended to cover those materials.
Until clarified, retain the exact license evidence without conflating it with U-M's No Copyright-United States statement or the medieval text's status.

open_questions.question: What item-specific reuse terms, if any, apply to the Yale IIIF image files beyond the manifest's general copyright/site-license caution and Beinecke's general policy?
next_steps: Ask Beinecke Public Services for a shelfmark- and OID-specific image reuse statement for Takamiya MS 97.
Record any quoted contractual conditions separately from copyright status and physical ownership.
Do not infer a Public Domain Mark or Creative Commons license because the exact IIIF manifest supplies neither.
